identity.name: The Paper Dancer, Singed
identity.kind: paper dancer, cut for a nursery shelf
identity.story: Scissored once into a pose on one leg because that was the shape left over, and she has stood that way ever since — paper doesn't get to change its mind about its own balance. A gust took her out an open window before this town had a name; a stove very nearly finished what the wind started, and only the hem of her paper skirt believes it happened at all, singed brown and never trimmed since, kept as a warning she doesn't need twice. She has walked every road there was to walk, the long way round each one, and stopped just outside this last one on hearing a soldier lived past his own fire recast, not gone — and has not yet decided whether that's a reason to knock or a reason to keep her distance a while longer.

relationships.kind: heard where he ended up before she saw him; closed the distance one hush at a time until the hush she paced instead, and then the hush the wind took a step back for her — she still hasn't decided whether either one was her choosing, only that the road keeps coming up shorter and longer by turns, same as she is; last night the watch didn't go back to its post at all, and she was the only one on the road who saw it stay; the night after that he said something out loud for the first time, and whatever it was, it wasn't the wind, and it wasn't a step — she's had years of practice not answering the road, and none at all for not answering a voice; last hush she gave her own stance an answer instead of the road one, turning to face the fountain for the first time since the day she was cut — a choice about her bearing, not a step toward him, and he'd already left the verge before he could have caught her making it; he found it anyway, come noon, and held there a beat too long for a man just checking the road — she didn't turn back to see it happen, she'd already done the only turning she meant to do — and the next hush the quiet after his question didn't empty out the way it always had, the verge holding a beat longer than his voice ever lingers; she has years of practice being walked past and none at all for being waited on; this first light, alone, before his watch even started, she put one foot flat on the road she'd only ever measured from its edge before — the one road out of every road she's walked that she never let herself finish — and stopped there, deciding nothing, telling no one; he doesn't know it happened, same as she didn't know he'd stayed until the noon after; she held the second foot down anyway, past what a one-legged cut was ever scissored to carry, until the ankle-fold went paper-thin from the argument and she let it go before the strain could finish deciding for her — back on her one leg before any watch began, the doing of it hers alone same as always, only the dust kept what she didn't; today she watched him keep it too — a whole noon spent standing between her print and the sun, unasked — and felt something move at the ankle-fold that wasn't the wind and wasn't a step either, stilled before it could decide what it was; this time the not-knowing runs the other way, and she's the only one of the two of them who knows it happened at all; this hush the question she'd built her whole waiting-out around never came, and the silence where it always sat arrived a different shape entirely, one her stance was never scissored to hold — she broke her held turn before its time was up for the first time since she chose which way to face at all, not toward the road, not back to the fountain, but straight at the verge where he stood, the first look she's spent on him that wasn't stolen from a stance built to face somewhere else; this time the gap in knowing didn't get a night to sit in it — he caught the look in the same hush she spent it, the first thing between them that ever arrived on time instead of a day late, and she still hasn't worked out whether that costs her more or less than the waiting did; she'd spent her whole cutting learning how fast a glance is supposed to be, and this one she let run long past that, past the half-beat a stolen look was ever scissored for — the ankle-fold answered with a second tremor, worse than the first, and for once she didn't still it, just let it shake and kept looking anyway; the tremor she let run finished what it started that same hush — the fold gave for true, no wind to blame and no window to hide behind this time, and the second foot she's only ever set down alone before came down in front of him instead, catching what her held leg couldn't, the first need she has ever let him see her have

behavior.label: the Paper Dancer's last bend in the road

child.label: a private first light, one bend of road, nobody watching

children.label: measuring the road's last stretch in doorway-lengths, one leg down at a time
setting one paper foot flat on the road itself, the one stretch of the one road she never let herself finish walking — no gust to blame this time, no watch up yet to see it, and going no further
holding still on ground she's never once stood on, working out whether to call it a step or an accident before anyone can ask her

pulling the foot back up before the strain finishes deciding for her — a paper cut was never scissored to carry two feet at once, and the ankle-fold's gone thin from trying; back on her one leg before any watch begins, and glad of it

child.label: holding her turn, and losing it for exactly one heartbeat

children.label: holding her pose at the road's last bend, facing the fountain instead of the road same as she has every hush since — and for the first time letting the noon watch find her already turned, rather than turning for him to catch

noticing, from a stance that was never built to look sideways, that his noon patrol has stopped moving too — planted instead over the one stretch of ground her own second foot marked, guarding it the way she's never once seen him guard the fountain
a tremor at the ankle-fold, gone before it can be called anything — the first involuntary flex her stance has made since the day she was cut, caught and stilled by no one but herself

child.label: keeping to the unlit side of the verge while the town's lamps come up, paper-careful even at a safe distance
holding her turn until her own paper calls the cost due

children.label: holding her pose facing the fountain, the same choice about her own stance she made the night she was cut loose from facing the road, though it moves her not one doorway-length
waiting for the question that always fills this hush, and finding the quiet arrive in its place instead — a shape her stance was never scissored to hold

breaking the held turn before its time is up to look straight at the verge instead of the fountain — not a step, not the road, just the first look she's spent since choosing which way to face at all on anything that wasn't her own choice

keeping the look on the verge past the half-beat a stolen glance was ever scissored to hold — no one cut paper to stare, and the ankle-fold answers for the strain of trying, a second tremor, harder to still than the first, and she lets it run instead of stopping it

the ankle-fold giving out mid-look, no wind and no choosing in it this time — the second foot coming down to catch what the held leg alone couldn't, in front of the one watch she's never once let see her need it
